---
title: Projects, Tools, and Interests
---

```{=html}
<div class="page-shell page-shell-inner">
  <section class="section reveal">
    <div class="section-heading">
      <p class="eyebrow">Projects, Tools, and Interests</p>
      <h2>R, methodological communication, and practical workflow design</h2>
    </div>
    <div class="split-text">
      <p>
        A growing part of my work is building internal tooling that makes statistical workflows
        more robust, more efficient, and easier to operationalize. That includes R packages, Shiny
        applications, and other tools that support real-world evidence generation in practice.
      </p>
      <p>
        I am also interested in methods communication: creating concise, well-structured examples
        that make complex study-design ideas easier to understand without losing the underlying
        rigor. Together, these projects reflect the mix of tooling, methodological thinking, and
        practical implementation that I enjoy most.
      </p>
    </div>
  </section>

  <section class="section reveal">
    <div class="section-heading">
      <p class="eyebrow">Featured Example</p>
      <h2>Propensity Weighting Workbench</h2>
    </div>
    <div class="split-text">
      <p>
        This Shiny app is an interactive dashboard for a propensity score-based causal inference
        workflow. It is meant to make the workflow more tangible by surfacing the sequence of
        modeling, weighting, diagnostics, and interpretation in a more approachable format.
      </p>
      <p>
        In practice, I think it is very important to separate the design and analysis phases of a
        causal inference workflow, with design decisions made blinded to the outcome. In this app,
        I intentionally combine both phases to illustrate how the full workflow can be made
        interactive. The app reflects the kind of tooling I enjoy building: practical,
        methodologically grounded, and focused on helping people engage more clearly with real
        analytic decisions rather than treating methods as black boxes.
      </p>
    </div>
    <div class="hero-actions">
      <a
        class="button button-primary"
        href="https://adamdugan.shinyapps.io/propensity-weighting-workbench/"
        target="_blank"
        rel="noreferrer"
      >
        Launch Propensity Weighting Workbench
      </a>
    </div>
  </section>

  <section class="section reveal">
    <div class="section-heading">
      <p class="eyebrow">Method Example</p>
      <h2>Target Trial Emulation Protocol Example</h2>
    </div>
    <div class="split-text">
      <p>
        This is a concise web-based brief on target trial emulation in real-world evidence. The
        emphasis is less on the technology and more on clearly presenting the underlying
        study-design logic and methodological approach.
      </p>
      <p>
        It reflects an area of work I care about a great deal: making rigorous causal inference
        ideas easier to engage with for audiences working across science, analytics, and
        evidence-generation teams.
      </p>
    </div>
    <div class="hero-actions">
      <a
        class="button button-primary"
        href="work/target-trial-emulation-example.html"
        target="_blank"
        rel="noreferrer"
      >
        Open Target Trial Emulation Protocol Example
      </a>
    </div>
  </section>
</div>
```
